narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 26, along with your upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend full-time, you've put significant effort into your law school application. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 78% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this tool to prioritize applicants likely to pass the bar and succeed in their programs. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is much lower than the typical student's rank around the middle of their class and signaled weaker performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score and Year 1 decile ranking, both also in the bottom decile compared to averages, further reinforced this concern by suggesting challenges in standardized testing and early academics. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, slightly below the average of 3.21 but still solid, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing. Similarly, your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans provided minor boosts, as they align with traits of many successful bar takers. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students, who tend to have stronger decile rankings and higher LSAT scores that predict bar passage more reliably. While this decision is disappointing, it highlights clear areas like improving test performance or academic rankings that the model flagged as hurdles. I encourage you to retake the LSAT to boost your score, seek academic advising to strengthen your class standings, or consider programs with preparatory support for the bar. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps and reapplying stronger. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing forward.
